edges.description: IGHMBP2 helicase deficiency leads to progressive degeneration of spinal anterior-horn lower (alpha) motor neurons, with a distal and diaphragmatic predilection.
edges.target: Anterior Horn Lower Motor Neuron Degeneration

edges.description: Degeneration of phrenic motor neurons produces neurogenic diaphragmatic palsy and respiratory failure, the early and life-threatening hallmark of SMARD1.
edges.source: Anterior Horn Lower Motor Neuron Degeneration

edges.description: Degeneration of distal-projecting lower motor neurons produces distal denervation, limb weakness, and amyotrophy beginning in the lower limbs.
edges.source: Anterior Horn Lower Motor Neuron Degeneration

nodes.description: Progressive degeneration of spinal anterior-horn lower (alpha) motor neurons, beginning before clinical symptom onset, with a characteristic distal-predominant and diaphragmatic/phrenic predilection. Motor neuron loss preferentially affects the distal muscles, particularly the lower extremities, while phrenic motor output to the diaphragm fails early.
nodes.id: Anterior Horn Lower Motor Neuron Degeneration

evidence.snippet: The initial symptoms of patients with SMARD1 are respiratory distress and distal muscle weakness manifesting in the infantile period due to progressive degeneration of α-motor neurons.

SMARD1 patients typically present with phrenic nerve palsy between the ages of six weeks and six months, and motor neuron degeneration primarily affects the distal muscles, particularly those of the lower extremities.
indicate that IGHMBP2 and SMN share common functions important for motor neuron maintenance and integrity in mammals

nodes.description: Absent deep tendon reflexes, usually established after the first year of life.

evidence.snippet: with an absence of deep tendon reflexes usually after the first year of life

nodes.description: Degeneration of the phrenic nerve and its motor neurons causes neurogenic diaphragmatic palsy, manifest radiographically as eventration (abnormal elevation) of the right or both hemidiaphragms—a highly suggestive sign of SMARD1—and clinically as respiratory distress and failure requiring mechanical ventilation between 6 weeks and 6 months of age. Unlike SMN1-related SMA, the thorax is normally shaped because the defect mainly involves the diaphragm.

evidence.snippet: Respiratory distress is usually the presenting clinical symptom and occurs between 6 weeks and 6 months of age as a consequence of the development of neurogenic diaphragmatic palsy.
the characteristic eventration (the abnormal elevation) of the right or, less frequently, both hemidiaphragms, which is considered a highly suggestive sign of SMARD1
it presents with respiratory failure due to diaphragmatic paralysis, progressive muscle weakness starting in the distal parts of the limbs, dysphagia, and damage to sensory and autonomic nerves

nodes.description: Abnormal elevation of a hemidiaphragm on chest imaging, a highly suggestive radiographic sign of SMARD1.

evidence.snippet: the characteristic eventration (the abnormal elevation) of the right or, less frequently, both hemidiaphragms, which is considered a highly suggestive sign of SMARD1

nodes.description: Distal denervation from lower motor neuron loss produces progressive wasting of the distal limb muscles—lower limbs affected earlier than upper—extending proximally with disease progression, with loss of deep tendon reflexes (areflexia), foot deformities, and progressive autonomic and sensory involvement.

evidence.snippet: The degeneration of the phrenic nerve is accompanied by the progressive wasting of the distal muscles of the limbs; the lower limbs are affected earlier than the upper limbs, and the proximal muscles become affected along with the progression of the disease.
progressive autonomic nervous system dysfunction also develops in association with the progressive worsening of motor functions in affected children

nodes.description: Progressive wasting of the distal limb muscles, lower limbs affected earlier than upper.

evidence.snippet: The degeneration of the phrenic nerve is accompanied by the progressive wasting of the distal muscles of the limbs; the lower limbs are affected earlier than the upper limbs, and the proximal muscles become affected along with the progression of the disease.

nodes.description: Progressive distal limb weakness beginning in the lower limbs and extending proximally.

evidence.snippet: The initial symptoms of patients with SMARD1 are respiratory distress and distal muscle weakness manifesting in the infantile period due to progressive degeneration of α-motor neurons.

nodes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in IGHMBP2 reduce or abolish the function of an ATP-dependent 5'->3' DNA/RNA helicase of superfamily 1 (UPF1-like) that associates with ribosomes in the cytoplasm and colocalizes with the RNA-processing machinery. The deficiency is thought to impair pre-mRNA processing and ribosomal translation/biogenesis required for motor neuron maintenance and integrity. Most SMARD1-causing missense mutations cluster in the helicase domain.

evidence.snippet: Here we demonstrate that SMARD type 1 (SMARD1) results from mutations in the gene encoding immunoglobulin micro-binding protein 2 (IGHMBP2; on chromosome 11q13.2-q13.4).
Like the SMN1 product, IGHMBP2 colocalizes with the RNA-processing machinery in both the cytoplasm and the nucleus.
Biochemical characterization has determined that IGHMBP2 is an ATP‐dependent 5’→3’ DNA/RNA helicase.
Most SMARD1‐causing missense mutations have been found in the helicase domain

nodes.description: Mechanical ventilation and respiratory support for the neurogenic diaphragmatic palsy and respiratory failure of SMARD1. There is no approved disease-modifying therapy; artificial ventilation can prolong survival, and management focuses on ventilation, nutrition, and quality of life.

evidence.snippet: Artificial ventilation can prolong survival, but no specific treatment is available.

nodes.description: Infantile respiratory distress progressing to respiratory failure from neurogenic diaphragmatic palsy, typically requiring mechanical ventilation between 6 weeks and 6 months of age; the leading cause of early mortality.

evidence.snippet: Most children with SMARD1 do not survive beyond the first year of life due to progressive respiratory failure.
